advantages: Average O(1) insert, lookup, delete — the fastest general-purpose keyed access.
Decouples storage from key ordering; you don't sort to find things.
Memory overhead is usually acceptable for the speed.
Composable: hash tables underlie dicts, sets, OrderedDicts, caches, indexes and dedupe.
disadvantages: No useful order on its own (insertion order is a Python convenience, not a sort).
Worst-case O(n) per operation under pathological collisions or adversarial keys.
Resizes are O(n) — fine on average, but a latency spike when one happens.
Requires hashable keys; mutable objects can't safely participate.
